content:
# Luke Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 104-105 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_013_page_001.jpg, LUK_013_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And there were present at that very time some who reported to him concerning the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.

**2** And he answered and said to them, "Do you think that these Galileans were sinners beyond all the Galileans, because they suffered such things?

**3** No, I say to you; but unless you repent, you shall all likewise perish.

**4** Or those eighteen upon whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them — do you think that they were debtors beyond all the men who dwell in Jerusalem?

**5** No, I say to you; but unless you repent, you shall all in like manner perish."

**6** And he spoke this parable: "A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking fruit on it and found none.

**7** And he said to the vinedresser, 'Behold, these three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree and find none. Cut it down; why does it also render the ground useless?'

**8** And he answered and said to him, 'Lord, leave it also this year, until I shall dig around it and cast dung upon it;

**9** and if indeed it bears fruit in the coming year — but if not, you shall cut it down.'"

**10** And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbath.

**11** And behold, a woman having a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and she was bent together and was not able to raise herself up completely.

**12** And Jesus, seeing her, called to her and said to her, "Woman, you are loosed from your infirmity."

**13** And he laid his hands upon her, and immediately she was made straight and glorified God.

**14** And the ruler of the synagogue, being indignant because Jesus healed on the Sabbath, answered and said to the crowd, "There are six days in which it is needful to work; in these therefore come and be healed, and not on the day of the Sabbath."

**15** But the Lord answered him and said, "Hypocrites! Does not each one of you on the Sabbath loose his ox or his donkey from the manger and, leading it away, water it?

**16** And this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan bound — behold — these eighteen years, ought she not to have been loosed from this bond on the day of the Sabbath?"

**17** And when he said these things, all those who opposed him were put to shame, and all the crowd rejoiced at all the glorious things being done by him.

**18** He said therefore, "To what is the kingdom of God like, and to what shall I liken it?

**19** It is like a grain of mustard, which a man took and cast into his own garden; and it grew and became a great tree, and the birds of the heaven nested in its branches."

**20** And again he said, "To what shall I liken the kingdom of God?

**21** It is like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour until the whole was leavened."

**22** And he was going through cities and villages, teaching and making his journey toward Jerusalem.

**23** And someone said to him, "Lord, are those who are being saved few?" And he said to them,

**24** "Strive to enter through the narrow door, for many, I say to you, shall seek to enter and shall not be able.

**25** From the time when the master of the house shall rise up and shut the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock at the door, saying, 'Lord, open to us,' and he answering shall say to you, 'I do not know you, whence you are' —

**26** then you shall begin to say, 'We ate before you and drank, and in our streets you taught.'

**27** And he shall say, 'I tell you, I do not know you, whence you are. Depart from me, all you workers of unrighteousness.'

**28** There shall be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth, when you shall see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves being cast out.

**29** And they shall come from east and west and from north and south, and shall recline in the kingdom of God.

**30** And behold, there are last who shall be first, and there are first who shall be last."

**31** In that very hour certain Pharisees came, saying to him, "Go out and depart from here, for Herod wishes to kill you."

**32** And he said to them, "Go and tell that fox, 'Behold, I cast out demons and perform healings today and tomorrow, and on the third day I am perfected.'

**33** Nevertheless I must go on my way today and tomorrow and the following day, for it is not possible that a prophet perish outside of Jerusalem.

**34** Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets and stoning those who have been sent to her — how often I wished to gather your children together, in the way a bird gathers her own brood under her wings, and you were not willing!

**35** Behold, your house is left to you. And I say to you, you shall not see me until the time comes when you shall say, 'Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.'"

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 104-105) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 12:46 through Luke 13:8 across four columns, with Luke 13:1-2 beginning in the rightmost column. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 13:9 through Luke 13:35 across four columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΕΝ ΑΥΤΩ ΤΩ ΚΑΙΡΩ (13:1, "at that very time" — linking to the preceding discourse)
- ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΟΙ ΠΑΡΑ ΠΑΝΤΑΣ ΤΟΥΣ ΓΑΛΙΛΑΙΟΥΣ (13:2, "sinners beyond all the Galileans")
- ΟΦΕΙΛΕΤΑΙ (13:4, "debtors" — distinguished from "sinners" in v. 2)
- ΑΜΠΕΛΟΥΡΓΟΝ (13:7, "vinedresser" — compound of vine and work)
- ΚΑΤΑΡΓΕΙ ΤΗΝ ΓΗΝ (13:7, "renders the ground useless")
- ΚΥΡΙΕ (13:8, "Lord" — the vinedresser's address to the owner)
- ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΑΣΘΕΝΕΙΑΣ (13:11, "spirit of infirmity" — genitive of quality)
- ΣΥΓΚΥΠΤΟΥΣΑ (13:11, "bent together" — medical term for spinal curvature)
- ΑΠΟΛΕΛΥΣΑΙ (13:12, "you are loosed" — perfect passive declarative)
- ΑΡΧΙΣΥΝΑΓΩΓΟΣ (13:14, "ruler of the synagogue")
- ΥΠΟΚΡΙΤΑΙ (13:15, "Hypocrites" — plural, implicating all who share the reasoning)
- ΘΥΓΑΤΕΡΑ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ (13:16, "daughter of Abraham" — unique in the NT)
- ΚΟΚΚΩ ΣΙΝΑΠΕΩΣ (13:19, "grain of mustard")
- ΕΙΣ ΔΕΝΔΡΟΝ ΜΕΓΑ (13:19, "into a great tree" — Sinaiticus reading)
- ΑΛΕΥΡΟΥ ΣΑΤΑ ΤΡΙΑ (13:21, "three measures of flour")
- ΤΗΣ ΣΤΕΝΗΣ ΘΥΡΑΣ (13:24, "the narrow door")
- ΕΡΓΑΤΑΙ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ (13:27, "workers of unrighteousness")
- Ο ΚΛΑΥΘΜΟΣ ΚΑΙ Ο ΒΡΥΓΜΟΣ ΤΩΝ ΟΔΟΝΤΩΝ (13:28, "the weeping and the gnashing of teeth")
- ΤΗ ΑΛΩΠΕΚΙ ΤΑΥΤΗ (13:32, "that fox")
- ΤΕΛΕΙΟΥΜΑΙ (13:32, "I am perfected" — present passive)
- ΟΥΚ ΕΝΔΕΧΕΤΑΙ (13:33, "it is not possible" — NT hapax)
- ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ (13:34, double vocative of intense grief)
- ΑΦΙΕΤΑΙ ΥΜΙΝ Ο ΟΙΚΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ (13:35, "your house is left to you" — Sinaiticus omits ΕΡΗΜΟΣ)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **13:1 — "at that very time" vs. "at that very season":** EMET renders "at that very season" (preserving *kairos* as "season"), LOGOS "at that very time," PNEUMA "At that very time." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "time" is the natural English rendering of *kairos* in a narrative temporal clause; "season" (EMET) imports an extended duration not required by context.

2. **13:1 — "mingled" vs. "mixed":** EMET renders "mixed," LOGOS "mingled," PNEUMA "mixed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "mingled" carries a more formal register appropriate to the gravity of the sacrilege described; both "mixed" and "mingled" are faithful to *emixen*.

3. **13:2 — "suffered such things" vs. "suffered this way":** EMET renders "suffered such [things]," LOGOS "suffered such things," PNEUMA "suffered this way." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "suffered such things" is the formal equivalent; PNEUMA's "this way" is adequate but less precise.

4. **13:4 — "debtors" vs. "guiltier":** EMET and LOGOS render "debtors" (*opheiletai*), PNEUMA renders "guiltier." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "debtors" preserves the significant shift from "sinners" in v. 2, reflecting the Aramaic debt-sin equivalence essential to the passage's theology.

5. **13:4 — "who dwell" vs. "dwelling" vs. "living":** EMET renders "dwelling," LOGOS "who dwell," PNEUMA "living." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who dwell" renders the participial phrase in clear English relative clause form.

6. **13:5 — "in like manner" vs. "similarly":** EMET renders "similarly," LOGOS "in like manner," PNEUMA "in the same way." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "in like manner" distinguishes the adverb *hosautos* (v. 5) from *homoios* (v. 3, "likewise"), preserving the subtle variation between the two warnings.

7. **13:6 — "he spoke" vs. "he was speaking":** EMET renders "he was speaking" (imperfect), LOGOS "he spoke," PNEUMA "he told." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "he spoke" is the standard narrative rendering for parable introduction; the imperfect aspect is adequately conveyed by context.

8. **13:7 — "vinedresser" vs. "vine-dresser":** EMET renders "vine-dresser" (hyphenated), LOGOS "vinedresser," PNEUMA "vineyard keeper." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "vinedresser" is the established single-word form; EMET's hyphenation is unnecessary; PNEUMA's "vineyard keeper" loses the compound *ampelourgon*.

9. **13:7 — "render the ground useless" vs. "render-useless the ground":** EMET renders "render-useless the ground" (hyphenated compound), LOGOS "render the ground useless," PNEUMA "wasting the soil." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — natural English word order without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's paraphrase.

10. **13:8 — "Lord" vs. "Sir":** EMET and LOGOS render "Lord," PNEUMA renders "Sir." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Lord" preserves the theological ambiguity of *kyrie* in the parabolic context, consistent with SOPHIA's approach in prior chapters.

11. **13:9 — "bears fruit in the coming year" vs. "produces fruit next year":** EMET renders "bear fruit in the coming [year]," LOGOS "bears fruit in the coming year," PNEUMA "produces fruit next year." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the coming year" formally renders *eis to mellon* while "next year" (PNEUMA) is colloquial.

12. **13:11 — "bent together" vs. "bent double" vs. "bent-together":** EMET renders "bent-together" (hyphenated), LOGOS "bent together," PNEUMA "bent double." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "bent together" renders *synkyptousa* without EMET's hyphenation; "bent double" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but not precisely the Greek.

13. **13:11 — "raise herself up completely" vs. "straighten-up completely":** EMET renders "straighten-up completely," LOGOS "raise herself up completely," PNEUMA "stand up straight." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "raise herself up completely" renders *anakupsai eis to panteles* with full precision; "stand up straight" (PNEUMA) loses the completeness expressed by *panteles*.

14. **13:12 — "you are loosed" vs. "you have been released" vs. "you are set free":** EMET renders "you have been released," LOGOS "you are loosed," PNEUMA "you are set free." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "loosed" preserves the verbal connection to *lyei* (v. 15) and *lythenai* (v. 16), maintaining the wordplay essential to the Sabbath controversy argument.

15. **13:13 — "was made straight" vs. "was straightened-up":** EMET renders "was straightened-up," LOGOS "was made straight," PNEUMA "straightened up." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "was made straight" is clear and dignified; EMET's hyphenation is unnecessary.

16. **13:14 — "needful" vs. "necessary":** EMET renders "necessary," LOGOS "needful," PNEUMA omits the term. SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "needful" maintains the elevated register consistent with SOPHIA's established usage in Luke 10:42, 11:42, and 12:12.

17. **13:15 — "But the Lord" vs. "And the Lord":** EMET renders "And the Lord," LOGOS "But the Lord," PNEUMA "The Lord." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But" captures the adversative force of the Lord's response to the ruler's indignation.

18. **13:16 — "ought she not to have been loosed" vs. "was it not necessary that she be loosed":** EMET renders "was it not necessary [that she] be loosed," LOGOS "ought she not to have been loosed," PNEUMA "should not this woman... be released." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "ought she not" conveys the divine necessity (*dei*) in natural rhetorical question form.

19. **13:17 — "were put to shame" vs. "were being put-to-shame" vs. "were humiliated":** EMET renders "were being put-to-shame," LOGOS "were put to shame," PNEUMA "were humiliated." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "put to shame" renders *kateischynonto* without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's intensification.

20. **13:17 — "rejoiced" vs. "was rejoicing" vs. "was overjoyed":** EMET renders "was rejoicing," LOGOS "rejoiced," PNEUMA "was overjoyed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "rejoiced" is the clean narrative rendering; "overjoyed" (PNEUMA) intensifies beyond the Greek.

21. **13:19 — "a great tree" vs. "a tree":** EMET includes "a great tree," LOGOS "a tree," PNEUMA "a tree." Sinaiticus reads ΕΙΣ ΔΕΝΔΡΟΝ ΜΕΓΑ. SOPHIA follows EMET — "a great tree" preserves the Sinaiticus reading *mega* that LOGOS and PNEUMA omit.

22. **13:19 — "nested" vs. "lodged":** EMET renders "nested," LOGOS "lodged," PNEUMA "nested." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "nested" captures *kateskēnōsen* (literally "encamped/dwelt") with the avian imagery appropriate to the context.

23. **13:21 — "flour" vs. "meal":** EMET renders "flour," LOGOS "meal," PNEUMA "flour." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "flour" is the natural modern English equivalent of *aleuron*; "meal" is archaic in this usage.

24. **13:23 — "someone" vs. "a certain one":** EMET renders "a certain one," LOGOS "someone," PNEUMA "someone." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "someone" is the natural rendering; "a certain one" (EMET) is overly formal for the indefinite *tis*.

25. **13:24 — "for" vs. "because":** EMET renders "because," LOGOS "for," PNEUMA "because." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for" maintains the formal register of causal *hoti* consistent with SOPHIA's established style.

26. **13:25 — "master of the house" vs. "master-of-the-house":** EMET renders "master-of-the-house" (hyphenated), LOGOS "master of the house." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — unhyphenated, as with prior decisions regarding compound expressions.

27. **13:27 — "I tell you" vs. "saying to you":** EMET renders "saying to you," LOGOS "I tell you," PNEUMA "I do not know where you come from." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "I tell you" is the natural rendering of *lego hymin* in direct speech.

28. **13:28 — "being cast out" vs. "being cast out outside" vs. "thrown out":** EMET renders "being cast out outside," LOGOS "being cast out," PNEUMA "thrown out." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "being cast out" renders *ekballomenous* without the redundant "outside" (EMET) or the colloquial "thrown out" (PNEUMA).

29. **13:31 — "came" vs. "approached":** EMET renders "approached," LOGOS "came," PNEUMA "came." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "came" renders *prosēlthon* naturally; "approached" (EMET) is overly formal for the narrative context.

30. **13:31 — "depart from here" vs. "journey from here":** EMET renders "journey from here," LOGOS "depart from here," PNEUMA "Leave this place." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "depart" formally renders *poreuou* in the imperative context; "journey" (EMET) is too leisurely; PNEUMA paraphrases.

31. **13:33 — "I must go on my way" vs. "it is necessary for me to journey":** EMET renders "it is necessary [for] me... to journey," LOGOS "I must go on my way," PNEUMA "I must keep moving." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "I must" concisely renders *dei me*; "go on my way" captures the purposeful *poreuomai*.

32. **13:34 — "a bird" vs. "a hen":** EMET renders "a bird," LOGOS "a hen," PNEUMA "a hen." The Greek *ornis* can mean either "bird" or specifically "hen." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) for "hen" in the simile clause, but renders the initial description with "bird" following EMET to preserve the broader maternal imagery. Final rendering: "in the way a bird gathers her own brood" — using "bird" as the more inclusive rendering of *ornis* that preserves the Old Testament allusion to divine wings (Deut 32:11; Ps 91:4).

33. **13:34 — "you were not willing" vs. "you did not wish it":** EMET renders "you did not wish [it]," LOGOS "you were not willing," PNEUMA "you were not willing." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "you were not willing" renders *ouk ethelesate* with the weight of settled refusal; "you did not wish it" (EMET) softens the confrontational force.

34. **13:35 — omission of "desolate":** Sinaiticus omits ΕΡΗΜΟΣ ("desolate"). EMET and LOGOS correctly follow Sinaiticus in omitting it. PNEUMA includes "desolate," departing from the source manuscript. SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS — the Sinaiticus reading is followed faithfully.

35. **13:35 — "until the time comes when you shall say" vs. "until it shall come when you say":** EMET renders "until it shall come [when] you say," LOGOS "until the time comes when you shall say," PNEUMA "until the time comes when you say." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "until the time comes when you shall say" renders the temporal clause with clarity while maintaining the future tense.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
